speaker: interviewer
text: Five interviewing Gerald Grossman. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I got on this locomotive, there was a whole bunch of railroad people all traveling towards the former border of Russia and Poland, which was about the area of Zhytomyr, a small town of Zhytomyr. And from there, we were traveling toward Kiev. All this, I didn't know what's going on. I mean, wherever the railroad was going, wherever they were pulling the people, that's where I was going-- with them. Slowly, we traveled through all of Ukraine. And the Russian government, I suppose, decided to gather all the railroad people that were behind because they call it [NON-ENGLISH] in the Ukraine, a Ukraine part of that one that was already being taken over by German-- by Germany. So they kept us behind. We were in Kiev. We passed Poltava, Kharkov, and from there we went back and forth because they are also gathered other railroad people, and they joined our transport. Where the Russian government decided to keep this whole transport of railroad people-- all of the areas that were taken by Germany, to hold them because they expected to go back. And-- and take back over the land that Germany took, and have the railroad people right away ready to fix the broken rails, the bombarded stations so they could bring the soldiers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as-- for the-- for about six months, five to six months, we've been on a train just traveling back and forth and back and forth. And while the Germans were coming, the Russians were retreating. And we landed in Moscow where they got out of all of the railroad people. And there was a plan by Stalin to make a railroad that goes around Moscow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was stationed in a monastery called-- in-- in the city of Zagorsk. And that's where we stayed while we were working to build a railroad that goes all around Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your particular job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just digging, bringing earth, putting up rails, and making the-- the-- the station. We made it. And on that rail, it was called [NON-ENGLISH], which I suppose is still in existence today-- that's all to protect the city of Moscow. And they were cruising around Moscow with guns, the soldiers and everything, just to protect shooting at-- at the Germans. As a matter of fact, the Germans were very close by that railroad. They were 12 kilometers from Moscow when I was there. And they came, and they destroyed this rail. We fixed it again, and so on. After we got through fixing this railroad, they had no work for us anymore. So they decided to send us to Stalingrad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found out that the whole transport is going to Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was at the end of 1941. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was already cold, winter in Moscow is pretty cold. And I asked another few guys that were with me on the railroad whether they would like to go deeper into Russia. In order for us to go someplace, we didn't know where-- which transport goes where. When I was out on the station, I saw that the-- the cars from the Moscow underground, the metro, which I recognized, were standing outside. I figured these cars, which were very precious to the Russians, were going to go somewhere deep into Russia. So we decided, this group, to join this particular train and we traveled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rather than go to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rather than to go to Stalingrad. At that time, I didn't know what Stalingrad meant, which I found out later what Stalingrad meant. But we decided to go deeper into Russia. And this train went to a city of Kalinin, north, and we were sort of freezing because there was no heat. We made it to Kalinin. And because we had our uniforms on, we were free of traveling right between the cars, the sides, cabins, whatever we could find. And from Kalinin, we managed to get to Gorki. In Gorki there was a whole group of refugees running away from the-- from Ukraine. And people that were running with families and children and old people-- thousands. And I figured that they were going deep to Russia also. We joined them in-- into transport. And we were traveling for a long time until we reached Tashkent. Where the-- the Tashkent is Uzbekistan, and the temperature is warmer over there. But there were thousands of refugees in Tashkent and they had no place to stay. Most of them were sitting at the railroad station of Tashkent. There was no place to sleep. They slept on the streets. At night it gets damp and cold and the daytime is warm. People were suffering from typhoid. Hundreds of people were dying in the streets from the sickness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed for a couple of days in the station. It wasn't easy because many times, I found out that there was a window open in the back of a station where the-- the nurses quarters used to be. At night, when the nurses were gone, there was a tiny little room, about I would say, eight by eight feet. We-- I crawled through the window, went inside, slept over there, and in the morning, I used to get out. Many times I was sleeping, I couldn't get in there. I was sleeping among all the people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The station used to get so crowded that whoever found a spot to lie on the floor took it early enough. The rest of the people, because it was cold at night, and whoever could get in there, they were standing one behind the other, just leaning on each other and with their legs spread just to stay over the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was very scarce, couldn't get food. And there was [NON-ENGLISH], they call it, water. They were boiling hot water for people to drink at the station. And the bread, they used to [? give ?] if you had some kind of a document to show, they used to give you a couple of grams of bread for the day, whoever could get into the line. And that's how you existed. I decided, we were still about five people from the railroad, and I had my uniforms-- I was pretty dirty-- and I decided to go and see the chief of the railroad in Tashkent. It was called [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] in Russian. When I saw him, i-- I made an appointment. I went up to his office, and I said, we have five people that were just separated from the group of the railroad people in Ukraine somewheres in-- in-- in Moscow. And we're here for him to give us some work. Lucky, he decided to send us to a-- a factory, a railroad factory, which was in Tashkent. It was called [RUSSIAN]. And all five of us were working in this factory on the railroad for nine months. We were registered in the city. I worked as a machinist at one of the factories where they were making railroad ties. The other four were working, pushing those trees up to the top to put them into the machine to cut for those ties. So I had a pretty good job. There was some food because we had coupons to-- to go to a restaurant to eat something, not too much, but just to exist. We had a place to sleep. And-- and it was considering, for us, it was luck to be there. During that time, I was always running to the-- to the railroad station to look for refugees who were coming by. I was looking for my family at all times. Wherever I was, I always looked into the cars to see if maybe somebody from my city, somebody of my relatives is passing by. I didn't find anybody. But I-- I met some friends that also were looking for-- for family in the same transports that were passing by Tashkent. One day, my uncle, which came back from the front, he was wounded. And because he was a soldier in the Russian army, taken right away when the war started, and they were defeated. He was full of wounds from the trenches that he was lying, fighting. He came with a group that he had, that wanted to go to the Polish army to sign up to the Polish army, Sikorski army, which was formed at a time in Russia. The Russian government gave him permission to gather all the Polish people and then go and fight from the English side.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was traveling. He passed by Tashkent. When he walked out, he met this friend of mine. And he asked whether there were any relatives or any friends from our city of Rovno. And he said there was, yes, there's one guy comes by, his name is Gedale Tennenbojm. He said, [? Gedale ?] Tennenbojm? That's my sister's son, where is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Tashkent is one of the biggest cities in Asia, in Uzbekistan. He says, I don't know, but I know that he works someplace. He went to the Bureau, to the address named Bureau they called it in Tashkent. He asked for my name, I was registered, and sure enough, he found my name where I worked. And he showed up in this factory in the daytime. I wasn't there in the daytime. I worked nights. In the daytime, I used to go around looking for relatives, looking to search for some-- some. Well, lucky enough, this day I came back to the place that I used to sleep. And the caretaker said to me that two of your brothers are looking for you. They were in Russian uniforms, soldiers. And I said, I had no brothers. I was the oldest one. So I-- I wouldn't have brothers that would be in the Russian army. And they said-- she says to me, that they are going to where you work. Well, I rushed to the place. And from afar I saw two soldiers sitting. I couldn't recognize them. And one of them was my uncle. Naturally he was pale and full of wounds. He didn't eat for three days traveling. And right away, I took him into the restaurant over there. I had some coupons left, because you had to show them, and the both of them, he was a-- the other one was a friend of his who was also going to the Polish army. And the both of them went in, they ate a little bit, and they felt a little better. He tells me, you are going with me. Now we are going to sign into the Polish army. And we won't-- I won't let you go now that I found you. I see you going with me. I said to him, I'm sorry. I can't go. I have a job. They have 40 people working on top there. I am the machinist. If I don't show up, 40 people won't be working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says, I can't let you go. I can't let you stay here anymore. You're going to have to go with me. Well, I decided to go with him. And I left the job without informing anybody. We-- from there on, I slept on the station again. The next morning there was a transport going to Guzar, near the Afghanistan border where the Polish army was assembling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we traveled all the way through the desert, practically, to go to this place of Guzar. When we-- when I left my job, I had a friend that was working there. The next morning he came to see me. He was the only one. His name was [? Schtekl. ?] He came to tell me that the work wasn't going on all night, that somebody took over the machine to work on it, and they overloaded it, and they burned the-- the-- the engine, the motor that was working, electric motor that was working for this purpose. And they're looking for me. Well, you can imagine how I felt. While I was traveling on the way to Guzar, in one of the towns before we reached the city, the end cover there, the KGB, came to the station and surrounded the whole station and wanted to see the documents, what everybody had. I knew what was happening. And being that I ran away from work to join the Polish army, I had to go into the toilet and destroy all documents that I had. My name was Tennenbojm at that-- at that time. And this was the last time that my name was Tennenbojm. I took some kind of a name that my uncle had from the group of 15, and not all 15 were there already, to go to Guzar. And I destroyed all my pictures, pictures I had from my family with me and my documents. When I came to Guzar, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to go through a inspection, like a medical inspection for the Polish officers that were there to see who is strong enough to go into the army. Well, this place, Guzar, it was a field. There were no women, just men. They undressed in the open air. It was hot, very hot over there. They undressed all the men, put them in a line, and they-- and that was the examination, the medical examination for the Polish people. When they saw-- the Jews they could recognize, because they were circumcised. And the Polish people were not. And they refused to take me and my uncle to the Polish army. And we were sent back. They went through Iran, Iraq, to Palestine. We had to go back into-- to Russia. Well, at that time, I took my uncle's name and I considered myself as a brother to him. And the name was, which was my mother's name, Gresores. So from there on I became Greshe Gresores. Gresores. Now we had to go back into Russia deep, but we landed in Jambul. It was a big city in Kazakhstan. Over there, the undercover there, the KGB again surrounded when they saw men on the streets, they picked them up. And my uncle and I were picked up again on the market. We were looking for something, for some food. They picked us up and they sent us to work. And they called it a Trudovaya armyia, which is our army for work. And they sent us to a kolkhoz not far from Jambul, I would say about 30 kilometers. And the kolkhoz, was-- the name of it was [? Emini ?] [? Molotov. ?] [? Molotov. ?] That was the foreign minister's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a kolkhoz?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kolkhoz is a collective farm. And we had to help them work on the fields until something happened over there. That two Polish guys, they were working there for some kind of iron works, and one of them killed a Kazak in the middle of the night. And they were afraid of us, all the people, the native people from that kolkhoz. And they had to-- they kicked us out from that kolkhoz. They didn't want us there anymore because they didn't know who was the one that killed that particular woman, I think it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back to Jambul from over there. And in Jambul, I found a friend that was a-- he was from Lvov. His name was Isaac [INAUDIBLE]. He was a very good mechanic and they-- the pulled us, the whole group of us, they took us to another place, which was called [INAUDIBLE], another collective farm where they needed mechanics to work and put together cars and fix their equipment and stuff like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being that I was a machinist and he was a mechanic from Czechoslovakia, he-- he-- finished-- he worked for the Skoda factory in Czechoslovakia. He was a very good mechanic. He took me in and he taught me mechanics. I was working there as an automobile mechanic and my uncle was working in the bread factory. Not a factory but a bakery, sort of. Well, being that he worked as a-- in the factory, we had some bread to eat. And other things, I worked in the-- in the factory so it wasn't too bad as far as food is concerned in that particular place. After, I think that this was in Jambul in 1943 sometimes, the beginning of '43. I worked there for a-- a short while, where an order came that my uncle should be taken to the Russian army because they needed more people for Stalingrad. He didn't want to leave me over there by himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I joined him, and we traveled. They picked us up, and they didn't take us to the army which they said they were gonna-- they gonna do it. Instead, they took us to [INAUDIBLE]. This was another part of Kazakhstan, not far from Akhtubinsk, a place where they were digging chromium. There were ores, iron ores, and they had, over there, a political prison, Russian political prison, 200 kilometers from Akhtubinsk. No towns in between, it was like in the wilderness. And that's where I spend the rest of the time until the war ended in 19-- I was still there until 1946. Can you tell us why you were there until 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were a group of people sent to work over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any kind of work, fixing houses, delivering stuff to the-- I-- I had a pass to go into the camp, not camp, but the prison. The minimum of that prison was 25 years for political prisoners, Russian political prisoners. And I used to transfer materials from the prison into the part where we used to live. We were sort of free. But you couldn't go any place. It was a small town, very small town that they used to work for that particular prison. Everything in that town was to support the prisoners. So they had some civilian people working there. But being there were no men, so they took us as the refugees, in Russia, the Polish people, and they send us there. It was a terrible place. The climate was very bad. Out of the 200 people they brought over there, only 20 remained. I was one of the 20. I myself was-- I-- I was sick with malaria in this particular place where every year in May, I was in the hospital for one month with chronic malaria that I picked up over there. The rest of the people from the 200, they sent them back. Some got very sick. And they send them back into deep Russia. What happened to them, I don't know. Just about 20 people remained in this place until the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we start again, will you tell us a little bit about were you-- was there a doctor there who diagnosed you as having malaria? Were you aware that you had malaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a hospital--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold-- hold all that. You know? We--
